title: Conjecture 3 (Nýdl's Conjecture)
statement_text: For any $n \geq 4$ and $\ell \geq \lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$, any two trees on $n$ vertices with the same $\ell$-deck are isomorphic.
context_text: Nýdl conjectured in 1990 that no two non-isomorphic trees have the same $\ell$-deck when $\ell$ is slightly larger than $n/2$. The conjectured bound would be sharp: Nýdl presented trees for which $\ell \geq \lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$ is necessary. There had been no progress on this conjecture since 1990, with the best previous result being Giles' 1976 bound handling only $r \leq 2$.
paper_contribution: Proves that any $n$-vertex tree $T$ can be reconstructed from its $(n-r)$-deck for all $r < n/9 + o(n)$ (Theorem 4), making substantial progress toward the conjecture and vastly improving the previous best result. Also notes the conjecture is false for $n = 13$ but verified true for all $4 \leq n \leq 25$ with $n \neq 13$.
notes: PDF source — math notation reconstructed from text. Conjecture is explicitly labelled 'Conjecture 3 (Nýdl [31])' in the paper.

title: Conjecture 1 (Kelly–Manvel Conjecture on Reconstruction from Smaller Cards)
statement_text: For every $r \in \mathbb{N}$, there is an integer $N_r$ such that every graph with at least $N_r$ vertices is reconstructible from its $(n-r)$-deck.
context_text: Reconstruction from small cards is generally attributed to Kelly, with this strengthened formulation due to Manvel, who calls it 'Kelly's Conjecture'. Kelly and Ulam's classical Reconstruction Conjecture posits $N_1 = 3$; this conjecture asserts that for any fixed $r$, sufficiently large graphs are always reconstructible from the $(n-r)$-deck.
paper_contribution: Contributes to the case of trees by proving (Theorem 4) that any $n$-vertex tree can be reconstructed from its $(n-r)$-deck for $r < n/9 + o(n)$, confirming the conjecture holds for trees with an explicit linear bound on $r$.
notes: PDF source — math may be garbled. Explicitly labelled 'Conjecture 1 ([19, 27])'; attribution year 1974 refers to Manvel's formulation [27], with Kelly [19] (1957) as the originator of the small-cards framework.

title: Spinoza–West Connectedness Threshold Belief
statement_text: For $n \geq 6$ and $\ell \geq \lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$, the connectedness of an $n$-vertex graph $G$ is determined by $\mathcal{D}_\ell(G)$.
context_text: Spinoza and West constructed two graphs $G_1 = P_n$ and $G_2 = C_{\lceil n/2 \rceil+1} \sqcup P_{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor - 1}$ which share the same $\ell$-deck for all $\ell \leq \lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ yet differ in connectedness, showing the threshold $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$ would be sharp if the belief holds. Spinoza and West proved connectedness is recognisable for $\ell \geq n - (1+o(1))\sqrt{2\log n / \log\log n}$.
paper_contribution: Proves (Theorem 5) that the connectedness of an $n$-vertex graph can be recognised from $\mathcal{D}_\ell(G)$ provided $\ell \geq 9n/10$, giving a linear gap and significantly improving the Spinoza–West bound.
notes: PDF source — stated in the paper as 'Spinoza and West believe that…', not a formally named conjecture. Math notation reconstructed from PDF.
